data.title: Columbia Sportswear faces $35M to $40M tariff headwinds, lowers FY25 guidance
data.description:
Facing macroeconomic uncertainty due to global trade policies, Oregon-based Columbia Sportswear (NASDAQ:COLM) reinstated FY25 guidance – albeit with a limited outlook – that anticipates a potential sales decline of 1%. This overshadowed better-than-expected second quarter results and sent shares plummeting on Friday.

Shares are down more than 11% resulting in a nine-year low for the stock.

“The apparel and footwear industry is facing increasing tariffs, on top of already high existing duties. In this period of global trade policy uncertainty, we continue to take actions to mitigate the financial and operational impacts on our business. For the upcoming Fall 2025 season, our focus is delivering exceptional value to consumers, who are pressured by higher prices for many consumer goods, as well as keeping inventory and dealer margins healthy,” Columbia CEO Tim Boyle said.

With a significant amount of merchandise imported from Vietnam, Indonesia, India, and Bangladesh, as well as footwear from China, Columbia Sportswear (NASDAQ:COLM) expects a tariff headwind of $35M to $40M. Accordingly, the company lowered its sales guidance set last February to $3.33B to $3.40B, estimating sales will be up 1% to down 1% from a year ago. This compares to prior guidance of $3.40B to $3.47B (up 1% to up 3%), and at the midpoint of $3.36B, less than the consensus estimate of $3.40B.

For the current quarter, Columbia (NASDAQ:COLM) expects sales to be down 1% to 3% -- translating into a sales range of $903.9M and $922.5M -- and earnings of $1 to $1.20 per share, both of which are less than estimates of $934.3M and $1.31 per share, respectively. This outlook assumes tariffs of 10% for all countries, except China, which remains at 30% for the remainder of the year. Accordingly, Columbia (COLM) does not expect to import any finished products from China in 2026.  

Faced with an even higher tariff exposure later in the year, mitigation efforts to cushion this impact include price increases, vendor negotiations, and cost-cutting.

“We continue to take action to mitigate the risks and financial impact of higher tariffs, which represents the largest tax increase the company has faced in its history,” Boyle said on the company’s earnings call with analysts.

data.title: Exxon open to M&A possibilities to 'drive more value,' CEO Woods says
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM) is looking at opportunities to buy other oil companies, after losing its prolonged arbitration dispute over Chevron's $53B acquisition of Hess and a year after buying Pioneer Natural Resources for $60B, CEO Darren Woods said Friday on a call with reporters.

Exxon (NYSE:XOM) wants to acquire companies to create value through combining assets and expertise rather than simply buying to get bigger, Woods said.

The CEO did not specify any targets or types of assets Exxon (XOM) might buy, but said any deal must "drive more value for the combined shareholders, more value than either company on its own can achieve," as opposed to recent deals in the oil sector which he called "consolidation plays" that do not create real value for investors.

"Frankly, we're not interested in buying volume," Woods said of buying companies just to add barrels to its list of reserves.

The Exxon (XOM) CEO also said the Trump administration should use trade talks with the European Union to fight tough new climate and human rights regulations that threaten U.S. companies with "bone-crushing penalties."

Woods said the E.U.'s Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive threatens to entangle U.S. companies in bureaucracy, erode their global competitiveness, and run counter to President Trump's deregulation agenda.

The regulation, due to be phased in from 2027, would require E.U. and non-E.U. companies with significant turnover in the bloc to ensure their supply chains do not harm the environment or
human rights.

Woods' comments came as Exxon (XOM) shares turned lower Friday, -1.5%, after reporting Q2 earnings that beat analyst expectations but fell 23% from the year-earlier quarter in the wake of lower oil prices.

data.title: Bull/Bear Debate: What does the future hold for Nvidia
data.description:
The heated debate surrounding Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) continues as the semiconductor giant pushes through geopolitical tensions to resume chip sales to China. This development has investors split between viewing it as a bullish catalyst or a concerning sign of market peak. The conversation centers on several key issues: Nvidia’s astronomical $4 trillion market capitalization, its ability to navigate complex geopolitical landscapes, and whether emerging competition from both China and American tech giants threatens its dominance in AI chip manufacturing.

Recent developments have fueled this debate, particularly the meeting between Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang and former President Trump, which resulted in a reversal of restrictions on chip sales to China. While this could potentially unlock billions in previously lost revenue, skeptics question whether this signals weakening future growth elsewhere and if Nvidia’s valuation already assumes unrealistic long-term performance. Meanwhile, the company continues expanding beyond chipmaking into energy infrastructure to address growing power constraints that threaten AI deployment, creating another dimension to evaluate its future prospects.

General Information

The Basics: Nvidia Corporation is a leading semiconductor company specializing in graphics processing units ((GPUs)) and system-on-chip units (SoCs) for computing platforms. The company has leveraged its GPU expertise to become the dominant provider of AI accelerator chips, with its products powering much of today’s artificial intelligence revolution. Recently, Nvidia has begun expanding beyond chipmaking into energy infrastructure development, working on advanced power delivery systems and an integrated “Energy OS” to address electricity constraints in data centers that threaten to bottleneck AI growth.

Competitive Landscape: Nvidia faces competition from established rivals like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) as well as emerging threats on multiple fronts. Chinese firms like Huawei are developing domestic alternatives, with some reports suggesting Huawei’s latest AI accelerator already surpasses the performance of Nvidia’s H800 chips in certain applications. American tech giants including Amazon and Apple are also developing custom AI chips to reduce their dependence on Nvidia. Additionally, geopolitical tensions between the US and China create ongoing uncertainty, despite the recent reversal of some export restrictions. According to US officials, China is only about two years behind the US in chip design capabilities, potentially threatening Nvidia’s market position in the future.

Quant Ratings: Seeking Alpha’s quant model currently rates Nvidia based on several key metrics. While comprehensive rating information wasn’t provided in the research, analysts are divided on the stock’s prospects. Bulls point to the company’s strong revenue growth and strategic expansion into energy infrastructure, maintaining “Strong Buy” ratings with price targets around $300. Bears highlight concerns about the sustainability of the $4 trillion market capitalization, with some estimating the intrinsic value closer to $67.72 per share, suggesting significant overvaluation.

Recent Stock Performance: Nvidia is currently trading at $179.70, having demonstrated remarkable growth with a year-to-date performance of +33.8% and a one-year performance of +73.3%. The stock has moved within a 52-week range of $86.62 to $183.30, currently trading near its 52-week high. This strong performance comes despite periodic concerns about geopolitical tensions and competition, reflecting continued investor confidence in Nvidia’s position within the AI ecosystem.

Bull/Bear Debate

Bulls emphasize Nvidia’s expanding business opportunities and strategic pivots that strengthen its market position. They highlight the company’s move into energy infrastructure as a crucial advantage in addressing AI’s real bottleneck: electricity. By developing an integrated “Energy OS” and advanced power infrastructure, Nvidia is positioning itself to capture additional revenue streams beyond chips. Bulls also point to the restoration of China sales as a significant positive, potentially worth $20-30 billion in demand for Nvidia’s H20 chips, though supply constraints may limit near-term impact.

Bears counter that Nvidia’s astronomical $4 trillion valuation is unsustainable even with aggressive growth projections. They argue that the company’s focus on resuming China sales may actually signal weakening growth elsewhere. Skeptics also highlight the rising risk of substitution, with both Chinese manufacturers and American tech giants developing alternatives to Nvidia’s chips. Some bears calculate that even if Nvidia were to make $200 billion annually for 20 years (far above current levels), the present value would only justify half its current market cap.

James Foord, Rating: Strong Buy: “On July 15th, the Nvidia CEO, Jensen Huang, visited the White House in order to meet with Trump. This meeting ended on a positive note for Nvidia. Trump referred to Jensen as his “friend” and reversed a Commerce Department decision back in April that restricted the sale of chips to China. This decision was estimated to cost Nvidia $5.5 billion. But now that it has been reversed, China is back on the menu for now.” - Will Trump TACO Nvidia?

Aseity Research, Rating: Strong Buy: “I rate Nvidia Corporation a Strong Buy because it’s one of the few AI vendors tackling the real constraint in the compute industry: electricity. The company is designing new 800V HVDC power infrastructure to support racks that could eventually approach one megawatt of power draw, expected in 2027. When those same racks are scaled up to a 100,000 unit hyperscale deployment, that’s roughly 100 gigawatts, or approximately 8% of the U.S. generation capacity.” - Nvidia: The Power Play Wall Street’s Blind To

Bears of Wall Street, Rating: Sell: “Despite the fairly good Q1 earnings report, Nvidia still faces major challenges that can make it harder for it to show a similar solid performance in the future. Although we now have a Sino-American trade truce, the United States still imposes major export restrictions on chips, which would cost Nvidia $8 billion in lost revenue in Q2 alone. That’s one of the reasons why the company’s CEO, Jensen Huang, is also pessimistic about the easing of chip rules by the United States.” - Nvidia: The Music Is About To Stop

Oriental Trader, Rating: Sell: “$4 trillion market cap is essentially saying equivalent to saying NVDA can earn net income of $400 billion a year in perpetuity, this would require $800 billion sales in perpetuity as well. Even if we set aside for a moment that NVDA is only at $150bn sales and $76bn net profit on a TTM basis and NVDA would need to achieve 400%+ growth just to reach those numbers… to put the $800 bn annual sales/$400 bn annual profit into perspective, the 5 biggest tech companies in America currently earn $437 bn in net income a year and would need to spend all of that on NVDA chips, every year forever.” - Nvidia: Why Resuming China Sales May Be Bearish, Signaling A Peak

data.title: Novo Nordisk gets FDA expanded approval for hemophilia treatment
data.description:
• Novo Nordisk (NVO) said it has received expanded approval from the FDA for Alhemo injection, as a treatment to help prevent or lessen bleeding episodes in both adult and pediatric patients aged 12 and older who have hemophilia A or B. 
  • This approval builds on the previous authorization granted in December 2024 for patients with hemophilia A or B who have inhibitors.
  • The approval is based on a late-stage trial data, which established the safety and efficacy of Alhemo

data.title: Jiangsu Easypark to acquire China Senior Living Industry International
data.description:
• Jiangsu Easypark Intelligent Technology announced on Friday that it has entered into a definitive agreement to acquire China Senior Living Industry International  (OTC:CHYL).
  • Upon completion, Jiangsu Easypark intends to consolidate its core assets into CHYL, creating a strengthened entity well-positioned to capitalize on growing global demand for smart city infrastructure.
  • As part of the post-transaction integration plan, CHYL's board of directors will be restructured. Jin Weiming is expected to be appointed as chief executive officer, assuming full executive leadership of the combined company.
  • CHYL is -0.0% to $0.1.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Consumer sentiment final print shows slight improvement from June
data.description:
July Consumer Sentiment: 61.7 vs. the prior July estimate of 61.8 and 60.7 in June, according to data released by the University of Michigan on Friday.

Year-ahead inflation expectations: +4.5% vs. prior print of +4.4% and +5.0% in June.

Five-year implied inflation: +3.4% vs. previous reading of +3.6% and 4.0% in June.

Current economic conditions index: 68.0 vs. 66.8 initial print and 64.8 in June.

Consumer expectations index was revised to 57.7 from the previous reading 58.6 and 58.1 in June.

data.title: ISM Manufacturing PMI contracts more than expected in July
data.description: The U.S. ISM Manufacturing PMI  slid to 48.0 in June, vs. 49.5 consensus, from 49.0 in the prior month, according to data released by the Institute for Supply Management on Friday.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: AMZN, FIG, ROKU, MRNA, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged down Friday morning as worries grew over the economic effects of President Trump's newly imposed higher tariffs ranging from 10% to 41%, affecting imports from over 90 countries, including key partners like Canada and India. Additionally, weak U.S. payroll data added to investor concerns about economic growth and monetary policy, contributing to the cautious market sentiment.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Figma (NYSE:FIG) +18% – Shares extended gains after a stunning NYSE debut on Thursday, where they soared 250% intraday, briefly topping $105. The design software firm priced its IPO at $33 per share, above the expected range, and saw its market value surpass $50B shortly after listing. The explosive rally marked one of the most dramatic IPO openings in recent memory.
  • Reddit (NYSE:RDDT) +14% – Shares rallied after the company delivered a double beat in Q2 and issued strong Q3 guidance. Daily active users rose 21% Y/Y to 110.4M, while revenue surged 78% to $500M, driven by 84% growth in ad sales. Q3 revenue is expected at $535M–$545M above the consensus of $472.7M with adjusted EBITDA of $185M–$195M (consensus $159.5M).
  • Kimberly-Clark (KMB) +7% – Shares rose despite Q2 earnings miss, as investors focused on the company’s highest volume growth in five years, which drove a 3.9% increase in organic sales even as total sales fell 1.6% Y/Y to $4.2B due to divestitures and FX headwinds. The company expects 2025 adjusted operating profit and EPS to grow at a low-to-mid single-digit rate on a constant-currency basis, though results will be weighed down by the PPE divestiture, U.S. private label diaper exit, and FX impacts, partly offset by a $0.16 per share tailwind from discontinued operations accounting.

Biggest stock losers

  • Coinbase Global (NASDAQ:COIN) -9% – Shares plunged after Q2 adjusted EPS of $0.12 fell far short of the $1.49 consensus, sharply down from $1.93 in the prior quarter and $1.10 a year ago. Total net revenue of $1.42B also missed estimates of $1.59B, declining from $1.96B in Q1 but up from $1.38B a year earlier. Looking ahead, Coinbase expects Q3 subscription and services revenue of $665M–$745M (midpoint $705M vs. $742M consensus), transaction expenses to grow in the mid-teens as a percentage of net revenue, tech and G&A costs of $800M–$850M, and sales and marketing spend of $190M–$290M.
  • Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) -7% – Shares declined even after the company topped Q2 expectations on revenue, EPS, and operating income, as its Q3 operating income guidance midpoint came in below consensus. Amazon expects Q3 revenue of $174.0B–$179.5B (midpoint $176.3B vs. $173.3B consensus) and operating income of $15.5B–$20.5B (midpoint $18.0B vs. $19.4B consensus). CEO Andy Jassy highlighted constraints in AI growth due to limited electricity and chip supply, noting capital expenditures of $31.4 billion in Q2 will likely persist for the remainder of the year as Amazon invests further in chips, data centers, and power infrastructure. 
  • Fluor (FLR) -29% – Shares plunged after the company missed Q2 estimates on both adjusted earnings and revenue and slashed its full-year guidance. Fluor now expects FY2025 adjusted EPS of $1.95–$2.15 (down from the prior view of $2.25–$2.75 and below the $2.58 consensus) and adjusted EBITDA of $475M–$525M (vs. the prior outlook of $575M–$675M), citing client hesitation amid economic uncertainty, delayed project awards, and continued pressure from legacy infrastructure projects. CEO Jim Breuer acknowledged short-term challenges but reaffirmed confidence in the company’s long-term strategy focused on disciplined execution and growth markets.
  • Roku (ROKU) -12% – Shares slumped despite better-than-expected Q2 results and the announcement of a $400M stock repurchase program, as investors focused on margin pressures. While Q3 revenue guidance of ~$1.2B (+13% Y/Y) topped the $1.17B consensus, and full-year revenue is expected to reach $4.65B (vs. $4.56B consensus), Q2 gross margins declined 0.9 pts Y/Y to 44.8%, driven by a 2.3 pt drop in platform margins to 51%. Roku raised its full-year 2025 outlook for platform revenue to $4.075B and adjusted EBITDA to $375M, citing strong free cash flow and improving profitability.
  • Moderna (MRNA) -7% – Shares slipped despite Q2 2025 results beating expectations, as the biotech trimmed the upper end of its full-year revenue forecast to $1.5B–$2.2B from a prior high of $2.5B. The cut was attributed to a timing shift in U.K. COVID vaccine shipments into Q1 2026. Moderna expects 40%-50% of H2 revenue to be recognized in Q3, with the rest in Q4, and reiterated its year-end cash and investments target of ~$6B.

data.title: Genius Sports deepens its bench in Europe with a new betting data contract
data.description:
Genius Sports Limited (NYSE:GENI) announced on Friday that it secured the exclusive official betting data rights for a select group of competitions within the European Leagues Association. As part of this multi-year partnership, Genius Sports (NYSE:GENI) will also deploy its GeniusIQ platform across European football.

The addition of European Leagues adds to the company's top-tier content portfolio that includes the Premier League and French Ligue 1. The European Leagues' members included in the agreement include theBelgian Pro League, the Ligová Fotbalová Asociace, the Danish League, the Dutch Eredivisie, the Dutch Keuken Kampioen Divisie, the Finnish Veikkausliiga, the Deutscher Fußball Bund, the Greek Super League, the Israeli Professional Football League, the Kazakhstan Football League, the Latvian Virsliga, the Norwegian Professional Football League, the Liga Portugal, the Romanian Professional Football League, tje Serbian Super Liga, the Slovak Niké Liga, the Föreningen Svensk Elitfotboll, and the Swiss Football League.

Shares of Genius Sports (GENI) opened with a 3.7% decline amid some broad market weakness. The sports data stock is still up more than 25% on a year-to-date basis and more than 60% over the last 52 weeks. Short interest on GENI stands at 12.4% of the total float.

data.title: Bayer gains after raising 2025 outlook
data.description:
Bayer (OTCPK:BAYRY) (OTCPK:BAYZF) shares traded higher in Germany on Friday after the company lifted its full-year guidance for currency-adjusted sales and earnings, citing a better-than-expected performance from its pharma division.

Announcing its preliminary Q2 2025 results, the company revised its 2025 currency-adjusted financial outlook for revenue and core earnings per share to €46B–€48B and €4.80–€5.30 from the prior forecasts of €45B–€47B and €4.50–€5.00, respectively.

The German conglomerate also added an extra €1.2B ($1.37) provision related to its ongoing Roundup (glyphosate) litigation in the U.S., mainly to account for an adverse ruling on its appeal in the Anderson et al. case.

The additional provision came after a Missouri appellate court in May upheld a $611M verdict awarded to three people who alleged that Roundup caused their cancers.

As for preliminary Q2 results, Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) reported €1.23 of core earnings per share on group sales of ~€10.7, driven by 2.2% YoY growth in its Crop Science Division, while its Pharmaceuticals and Consumer Health divisions expanded 0.6% and 0.2%, respectively, on an adjusted basis.
